title: SEGMENT 2 — Meet Mila — the clinical story of a healthy child becoming sick

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — Meet Mila — the clinical story of a healthy child becoming sick
So let me introduce you to the patient.
Because before this becomes a story about molecules and splicing and oligonucleotides, it is a story about a child.
Mila Makovec was born healthy.
By every account, by every measure her pediatrician had, she developed normally through the first three years of her life.
She walked on time.
She talked on time.
She was, to use the phrase that appears over and over in case reports of children with late-onset neurodegenerative diseases, "previously normal.
" Photographs from her early childhood look like every other child's early childhood.
There was no warning.
At about age three, the first thing went wrong.
Her left foot started turning inward when she walked.
A small thing.

The kind of thing a parent notices, a pediatrician examines, an orthopedist looks at, and most of the time it turns out to be nothing — a tight muscle, a developing arch, a minor orthopedic quirk that resolves on its own.

Sometimes it's the first sign that something is happening inside the developing nervous system, but you usually can't tell which one of those it is until time passes and you see whether other things start going wrong.
In Mila's case, other things did start going wrong.
Over the next two years — between ages three and five — Mila's clinical picture changed in ways that her family and her physicians watched with growing alarm.
Her vision began to deteriorate.
Not gradually-needing-glasses deterioration.
Real, progressive vision loss, the kind where a child who could see her parents' faces last year cannot see them this year.
She started bumping into furniture.
She would walk past toys on the floor she'd loved last week.
Examinations of the back of her eye — the retina — showed degeneration of the light-sensing cells.
Then her language began to regress.
This is one of the most chilling things in pediatric neurology — when a child who has been speaking, sometimes speaking quite well, begins to lose words.

Not the normal "I forgot how to say it" of a small child reaching for vocabulary they don't yet have, but the heartbreaking reverse — words she used to use, freely and correctly, simply stopped coming out of her mouth.
The vocabulary shrank.
Sentences shortened.
Eventually they almost stopped altogether.
She became clumsy.
Her movements lost their precision.
She had difficulty with the fine motor tasks she had previously been able to do.
And she began to have seizures.

By the time she was six years old — the age at which the milasen treatment began, and the age at which most of the clinical description in the paper was written — here is what Mila's life looked like, day to day.
She was blind.
She had what we call ataxia, which means her movements were uncoordinated; she could not walk without support.
She was having approximately thirty seizures per day.
Thirty.
Per day.
We will come back to that number, because the eventual measurement of how the drug affected those seizures is the cleanest piece of evidence in the entire paper that milasen was doing something real.
She had developed dysphagia — that's the medical term for difficulty swallowing — to the point that she required a gastrostomy tube.
A G-tube.
A surgically placed feeding tube that delivers food directly into her stomach because she could no longer safely eat by mouth without the risk of aspirating food into her lungs.
She was minimally verbal.
She was alert — and this is an important and painful clinical detail — she was alert.
The lights were on.
She was, by every neurological sign her doctors could measure, aware of the people around her, present in the room, recognizable as the same child her parents had always loved.
She just could no longer move or see or speak the way she used to.
An MRI scan of her brain showed atrophy.

Both the cerebrum — the big folded outer part of the brain that does most of what we think of as conscious thought, sensation, and motor planning — and the cerebellum, the smaller structure in the back that handles coordination and balance, had shrunken visibly compared to what a normal six-year-old's brain looks like.
The brain was, literally, losing tissue.
The doctors at Boston Children's took a tiny biopsy of her skin.
Why skin?
Because skin is easy to access and skin cells contain the same machinery — the same lysosomes, the same enzymes — as cells throughout the rest of the body.
They put the skin cells under an electron microscope and looked at the lysosomes, which are tiny compartments inside cells that we'll discuss in detail in the next segment.
And they saw something diagnostic.
Inside the lysosomes were dense, layered deposits arranged in patterns that pathologists describe with a vivid, almost poetic word: fingerprints.
Curvilinear, whorled deposits that look, under the electron microscope, like the swirls of a human fingerprint pressed into the cell.
These are not normal.

They appear in a specific family of diseases collectively called neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis — a mouthful, also called by the older eponymous name Batten disease — in which cellular waste material that should be broken down and recycled by the lysosomes instead accumulates and accumulates until the cell, eventually, dies.
The fingerprint inclusions are diagnostic for one particular subtype of Batten disease, the form caused by problems in a gene called CLN7.
So now her doctors knew, on clinical grounds, what category of disease they were looking at.
Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis, CLN7 type.
Batten disease.
A relentless, fatal, childhood neurodegenerative disorder.
The next step would be genetic confirmation — finding the actual DNA mutations responsible.
And that, as we will see, is where the case got strange.
And eventually, where it got remarkable.
